seasonal_wine.name: Bud Break and Spring Frost Risk
seasonal_wine.description: At 750-1,000 metres altitude, Ribera del Duero vines break dormancy in late March or early April, several weeks later than lower-altitude Spanish regions. Frost risk persists until mid-May and is the grower's primary spring anxiety. The Consejo Regulador tracks frost events; a late frost in early April can destroy 30-50% of young shoots in exposed parcelas. Visitors in late March and April see the meseta vineyards still leafless but the first green shoots emerging from pruned spurs.

seasonal_wine.name: Vine Flowering and Fruit Set
seasonal_wine.description: Flowering (floracion) typically occurs in June in Ribera del Duero, roughly 60-70 days after bud break. The ten-day flowering window is weather-sensitive: cold or rainy conditions cause coulure (uneven fruit set) and millerandage (uneven berry sizing), reducing yields and potentially harming wine quality. Fine warm Junes with low wind are celebrated in the DO's vintage reports as a predictor of even ripeness. Vineyards across the plateau carry a faint honey-like scent during this period.

seasonal_wine.name: Summer Drought and Heat Stress
seasonal_wine.description: Summer temperatures on the Castilian meseta regularly exceed 40 degrees Celsius during July and August, with annual rainfall averaging only 450 mm. Old bush-trained vines (en vaso) have deep root systems that access subsoil moisture, giving them drought resilience that trellised modern plantings lack. This is one reason pre-phylloxera ungrafted vines in Soria command a premium: their root systems have decades or centuries of adaptation to meseta drought. Climate change has extended summer heat stress, leading some producers to pick white Albillo Mayor in late August to preserve freshness.

seasonal_wine.name: Veraison: El Envero
seasonal_wine.description: Veraison - the moment grapes soften and shift from green to red - is called el envero in Spanish, giving the DO's premier wine awards event (Premios Envero) its name. In Ribera del Duero, veraison typically occurs in late July through August depending on altitude and clone. Higher-altitude parcelas in Soria reach veraison a week or more later than valley-floor plots in Valladolid. The moment is closely watched by growers as a predictor of the harvest window and potential sugar accumulation rate.

seasonal_wine.name: Albillo Mayor Harvest (White Wine Season)
seasonal_wine.description: Since the October 2019 DO rule change authorising white wines, Albillo Mayor grapes are harvested first, typically in late August or very early September. The grape requires precise timing: harvest too early and it lacks complexity; too late and it loses the acidity that defines Ribera's emerging whites. About 30 bodegas produce Albillo Mayor DO whites, most in limited batches under 5,000 bottles. The best examples show citrus and stone-fruit aromas with a distinctive saline minerality from the calcareous soils.

seasonal_wine.name: Tinto Fino Harvest Window
seasonal_wine.description: The main Tinto Fino (Tempranillo) harvest in Ribera del Duero typically begins in late September and extends through mid-October. The exact date is set by the DO's Consejo Regulador based on sugar and acidity sampling from representative parcelas. Harvest is hand-picked on most quality-oriented estates, with mechanical harvesting used on cooperative-scale operations. The short cool nights of October - temperatures can drop to 5 degrees Celsius - allow for overnight transport of grapes without cold-chain equipment, preserving acidity naturally.

seasonal_wine.name: Gran Fiesta de la Vendimia
seasonal_wine.description: The DO Ribera del Duero's official harvest festival, the Gran Fiesta de la Vendimia, has been held annually in Aranda de Duero since 2018, reaching its eighth edition in 2025. Organised by the Consejo Regulador in collaboration with the Ayuntamiento of Aranda de Duero, it runs over three to four days in early September, featuring winery booths in the Plaza Mayor, traditional grape-treading ceremonies, live music, and gastronomy showcasing the region's lechazo (roast suckling lamb). Multiple Ribera del Duero bodegas pour current releases for tasting ticket holders.

seasonal_wine.name: Roble and Crianza Releases
seasonal_wine.description: DO Ribera del Duero regulations require Crianza wines to spend a minimum of 12 months in barrel and 12 months in bottle before release. In practice most Crianza wines from a given vintage reach the market in the spring of the second calendar year after harvest. Roble (informal oak-aged category) releases come earlier, typically six to ten months after vintage. Spring tastings at bodegas in April and May are the prime window to taste newly released Crianza and current-vintage Roble from a range of producers.

seasonal_wine.name: Reserva and Gran Reserva Release Cycle
seasonal_wine.year_range: Autumn (3rd year after harvest) and beyond
seasonal_wine.description: DO regulations require Reserva wines to have a minimum 12 months in barrel and 24 months in bottle; Gran Reserva requires 24 months in barrel and 36 months in bottle. Reservas from a given vintage typically reach market in the autumn of the third year after harvest. Gran Reservas arrive a further two or more years later. Vega Sicilia's Unico, which may spend 10 years or more in a combination of barrel and bottle before release, is not released on a standard vintage cycle but only when deemed ready by the estate.

seasonal_wine.name: Premios Envero Wine Awards
seasonal_wine.description: The Premios Envero, reaching their 26th edition in 2025, are the DO Ribera del Duero's flagship consumer-facing wine awards, organised by the Ayuntamiento of Aranda de Duero. The format - 'mil siempre aciertan' (a thousand always get it right) - uses 1,000 tasters, both amateurs and professionals, to blind-assess submitted wines from the DO. In 2024, 89 wineries entered 248 samples. Winners are announced at the EnveroFest outdoor event held at Parque de la Isla in Aranda de Duero in late May, with a final awards gala following in early June.

seasonal_wine.name: Winter Dormancy and Pruning Season
seasonal_wine.description: Ribera del Duero winters are severe: temperatures regularly drop below minus 10 degrees Celsius on the meseta, and snow falls in December through February. Vine dormancy is complete, and pruning teams work through winter on both gobelet (en vaso) and trellis systems. Winery visits are quieter but cellar hospitality is warmer; several estates offer barrel tastings and library-wine access in winter when summer tourist pressure eases. The austere landscape of bare vines crossing the brown Castilian plateau under wide skies appeals to photography and contemplative tourism.

seasonal_wine.name: Vino + Tapas Event in Valladolid
seasonal_wine.description: The Ribera del Duero DO participates annually in the Vino + Tapas trade and consumer event held in Valladolid, typically in mid-May. Wineries set up along Calle Acera de Recoletos showcasing current releases alongside local tapas pairings. The 2025 edition ran 16-18 May. The event draws trade buyers and the general public alike and is an opportunity to taste Crianza and Reserva releases alongside younger Roble styles. It is one of the DO's most accessible consumer events for visitors based in Valladolid.
